metadata.title: Manuscript Reviewer Response: Claude as Adversarial Reader

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Hola, Liam — in for Bear. This is Liam, in for Bear? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: This is Liam, in for Bear?
props.segment: Manuscript Reviewer Response: Claude as Adversarial

beats.narration_text: Reviewer 2 had written four paragraphs of concern. The underlying argument was buried in jargon. She pasted the comments into Claude and asked: summarize the underlying concern, then draft a response paragraph explaining the methodology. Claude returned a structured draft — acknowledged concern, clear explanation, three supporting points. It was useful. One of the three supporting points described a methodological choice that did not match what she had actually done. She caught it. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, summarize reviewer concern and draft response

props.command: Here are Reviewer 2's comments — summarize the underlying concern, then draft a paragraph explaining why we used this methodology

beats.narration_text: Reviewer language and underlying concern are not the same. A reviewer who writes 'the sample is insufficient' may be asking about power, representativeness, recruitment bias, or generalizability — four different concerns requiring four different responses. Claude is useful here: ask it to name the underlying concern separately from the stated critique, then rank what the reviewer most likely wants addressed. The summary is not the response. It is the map before the response.
beats.new_visual_element: Underlying concern — stated critique ≠ underlying concern / power / representativeness / bias / generalizability / map before response
beats.spark_line: Name the concern before the defense.

beats.narration_text: The methodological claim in Claude's draft must be verified against your actual data before it enters the response. Claude generates plausible methodology descriptions based on what the kind of study you described typically does. It does not know what you actually did. 'We controlled for X by Y' requires checking that you controlled for X by Y. Sending a reviewer response with an incorrect methodological description is a professional error that Claude's fluency made easy to miss.
beats.new_visual_element: Verify against data — plausible ≠ accurate / Claude describes typical not actual / check before sending / professional error
beats.spark_line: Claude describes typical. You verify actual.

beats.narration_text: Claude is useful as an adversarial reader in one specific way: ask it to argue against your response from the reviewer's perspective, then identify the two strongest objections you have not yet addressed. This is rehearsal, not verdict. Claude cannot tell you whether your methodology is correct. It can show you where your response leaves an opening. The rehearsal is the value. The methodological judgment is yours.
beats.new_visual_element: Adversarial reader — argue against your response / two strongest unaddressed objections / rehearsal not verdict

beats.narration_text: The COPE authorship rule applies: you are responsible for all content in the response, regardless of AI assistance in drafting. Acknowledge before defending — that structure is correct and Claude produces it well. Verify every methodological claim against your actual study. Use the adversarial reader pass as a rehearsal before submission. Claude as a first draft and rehearsal partner is the correct use. Claude as the final authority on your methodology is not.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — COPE rule / acknowledge before defend / verify methodology / adversarial pass / you are responsible
beats.spark_line: First draft and rehearsal. Not final authority.

beats.narration_text: Use this three-pass workflow for your next reviewer response. Paste this.

props.command: Run three passes on these reviewer comments: (1) Summarize the underlying concern separately from the stated critique — name what the reviewer most likely wants addressed; (2) Draft a response that acknowledges the concern first, then explains the methodology; flag any methodological claim with [VERIFY] so I can check it against my actual data; (3) Argue against my response from the reviewer's perspective and name the two strongest objections I have not yet addressed. [paste reviewer comments]

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. The COPE authorship rule applies: you are responsible for all content in the response, regardless of AI assistance in drafting. Acknowledge before defending — that structure is correct and Claude produces it well. Verify every methodological claim against your actual study. Use the adversarial reader pass as a rehearsal before submission. Claude as a first draft and rehearsal partner is the correct use. Claude as the final authority on your methodology is not. Use this three-pass workflow for your next reviewer response. Paste this.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Manuscript Reviewer Response: Claude as Adversarial Reader] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
